planting.note: Divide established clumps in early spring or plant container-grown plants in deep, moist, humus-rich, fertile, slightly acidic soil in part shade; Przewalski's ligularia requires consistently moist soil and wilts dramatically in hot afternoon sun even when well-watered — site in dappled shade with good air circulation. The popular hybrid The Rocket (L. stenocephala x L. przewalskii) has similar cultural requirements but more heat tolerance.

directions: Plant in deep, moist, humus-rich, fertile soil in part shade; Przewalski's ligularia is one of the most architecturally spectacular shade moisture perennials, producing a dramatic combination of ornamental features rarely found together in one plant. The large, deeply and finely divided, almost feathery, palmate leaves — more intricately cut than those of other ligularias — are carried on long stems that form bold, tropical-looking clumps of striking textural interest. From these clumps in July and August arise tall, slender, black-stemmed flower spires — the distinctly dark purple-black stems are ornamental in themselves — bearing dense, narrow columns of small, bright-yellow, daisy-like flowers that create luminous, candelabra-effect spires against the shade. The black stems against yellow flowers is a stunning color combination. Native to northern China, it grows naturally along stream banks, wet meadow margins, and in moist mountain scrub. The hybrid The Rocket, crossed with L. stenocephala, is more heat-tolerant and widely grown in warmer regions.

planting.note: Divide established clumps in early spring or plant container-grown plants in deep, moist, humus-rich, fertile, neutral to slightly acidic soil in part to full shade; astilboides requires consistently moist to wet soil and should never be allowed to dry out. It is slow to establish but becomes impressively bold once settled. Protection from wind is important as the enormous leaves can be damaged by strong gusts.

directions: Plant in deep, moist to wet, humus-rich, fertile soil in part to full shade; tabletop astilboides is grown for what may be the most dramatically impressive foliage of any hardy perennial — individual circular, peltate (the stem attaches to the center of the leaf, like an umbrella handle) leaves that reach 2-3 feet across in mature specimens, creating enormous, flat, table-like platforms of bold textural interest in the shade garden. The leaf surface is light green, somewhat hairy, with prominent lobes and veining visible across the whole expanse. In early summer, the plant produces tall stems bearing large, branching panicles of small, cream-white flowers above the foliage, adding floral interest to the already spectacular leaf display. Native to moist valleys and stream margins in northeastern China and Korea, it requires consistently moist, fertile, humus-rich soil in a shaded position — drying out causes the enormous leaves to scorch rapidly. An irreplaceable plant for creating a bold tropical effect in cool, moist shade gardens alongside hostas, ligularias, and rodgersias.

planting.note: Divide established clumps in spring or fall in moist, fertile, humus-rich, slightly acidic to neutral soil in part shade to full sun; bistort spreads steadily by rhizomes to form ground-covering colonies in moist conditions. The cultivar Superba is more compact and floriferous than the species, with larger, brighter-pink flower spikes. Bistort is also known as snakeweed from its twisted rhizome, and the young leaves and shoots have been used in traditional British cooking.

directions: Plant in moist, fertile, humus-rich, slightly acidic to neutral soil in part shade to full sun; bistort is a spreading, rhizomatous European meadow perennial producing soft, poker-shaped, bottlebrush-like spikes of densely packed, pale-pink to deep-rose-pink flowers in May and June — one of the loveliest and most distinctive flower forms of the early summer garden, resembling a soft, elongated pink bottlebrush rather than the typical flat daisy or spike flower. The flower color is a particularly soft, clean rose-pink that harmonizes beautifully with other cool-toned perennials. In moist, partly shaded conditions, bistort spreads steadily to form extensive, weed-suppressing groundcover colonies of attractive, broad, dock-like leaves. The cultivar Superba is the most garden-worthy selection, with larger, more intensely colored pink spikes above compact, dense foliage. Historically, the young leaves were used in the traditional English Lake District dish Dock Pudding. An outstanding plant for naturalizing in moist, partly shaded conditions near water features.

planting.note: Root softwood cuttings in summer or divide established plants in spring in well-drained, lean to moderately fertile, neutral to slightly alkaline soil in full sun; gold and silver chrysanthemum is valued primarily as a foliage plant and groundcover rather than for its flowers. The small, yellow button flowers appear very late — October-November — and may not fully open before frost in northern zones, but the foliage is ornamental from spring through fall.

directions: Plant in well-drained, lean to moderately fertile, neutral to slightly alkaline soil in full sun; gold and silver chrysanthemum is grown primarily for its exceptionally beautiful foliage rather than its flowers — the deeply lobed, dark forest-green leaves are edged and outlined with a neat, precise border of bright, silvery-white hairs that precisely traces every contour of each leaf lobe and margin, creating the striking two-tone appearance that gives the plant its common name. The contrast between the dark green leaf surface and the precise silver edging is quite extraordinary, particularly in autumn when cooler temperatures intensify both the green and the silver tones. The plant forms a low, mounding, spreading groundcover that is drought-tolerant and adaptable to a range of soils once established. Small, mustard-yellow button flowers appear very late in October and November and may not fully develop before frost in zone 5-6 gardens, but in warmer zones the late bloom adds an additional seasonal interest. Native to coastal cliffs and rocky slopes of Japan, it thrives in well-drained conditions in full sun.

planting.note: Divide established clumps in spring or plant container-grown aquatic plants in consistently wet, mucky, or submerged (up to 4 inches deep) soil in part shade to full sun; water forget-me-not is a true aquatic perennial that thrives at pond edges, in bog gardens, and in shallow water — quite different from the common woodland forget-me-not (M. sylvatica) which is a dry-garden annual. It spreads to form attractive floating mats in shallow water.

directions: Plant in consistently wet, boggy, or shallow-water conditions in part shade to full sun; water forget-me-not is the aquatic, perennial member of the forget-me-not genus — a genuine wetland native that grows naturally at pond and stream margins, in bogs, and with stems and bases submerged in shallow water, where it forms attractive spreading mats of trailing, bright-green stems with the same pure, sky-blue, yellow-eyed flowers familiar from the common garden forget-me-not, but from May into July and on a plant that returns every year rather than dying after seeding. The brilliant, clear blue of the small flowers is one of the most saturated, pure blues in the plant kingdom, providing a vivid focal point at the pond's edge throughout late spring and early summer. In shallow water features, the trailing stems and leaves float attractively on the surface while flowers arise above the water. An essential plant for naturalistic water gardens, rain gardens, and bog gardens where it softens the edges with a haze of blue flowers.
